IOCL Generic Cards — Designing the Fallback Without Making It the Default

48% of truck owners were failing to get an IOCL fuel card. The fix was a fallback path. The design challenge was making sure it stayed a fallback.

ROLESole product designer — app + ops portal
PLATFORMAndroid (Zinka app) + internal ops portal
SURFACESUser-facing flow + agent portal
01

What shipped

52% → 72%

Card creation success rate after generic cards were introduced — a 20-point jump without changing the primary flow

2 surfaces

Same fallback feature designed twice — once for truck owners on the app, once for ops agents on the portal. Different users, opposite risk profiles.

2.2%

Maximum rewards yield on generic card transactions, up from 0.6% — making the fallback commercially valuable, not just operationally necessary

02

The problem in numbers

Getting an IOCL FASTag fuel card through BlackBuck required the truck owner to go through a three-stage verification process — mobile number and KYC verification, IOCL portal entry, and then the actual card creation. Each stage had its own failure mode, and none of them were in BlackBuck's control.

23%dropped before portal entry — mobile number and KYC verification failures
17%dropped at IOCL API rejection — fuel KYC issues and IOCL-side rejections
8%dropped after CID creation — card creation rejected at the final step

Only 52% of interested users ever got a card. The rest hit a wall somewhere in the IOCL verification chain and had no path forward.

Generic cards solve this by bypassing CID creation entirely. Instead of issuing a card under the truck owner's identity, the card is created under BlackBuck's own CID. No mobile verification. No KYC check. No IOCL portal dependency. The card works immediately for fuel transactions.

The catch: because rewards are disbursed at CID level, any points earned on generic cards accumulate under BlackBuck's CID — not the customer's. This created the rewards problem that shaped much of the design work.

03

The question that shaped both designs

How do you give users a shortcut without encouraging them to take it?

A generic card is strictly a fallback. It exists for users who've genuinely hit a wall — whose OTP didn't arrive, whose KYC failed, whose IOCL application got rejected. It is not meant to replace the standard flow, which gives the truck owner a card under their own CID, their own rewards account, and full control over their balance.

But if the generic card path is easy to find and frictionless to use, both truck owners and ops agents will start reaching for it by default — not because they're being dishonest, but because systems shape behaviour more than instructions do. Tell an agent to only use the fallback for genuine exceptions, then make the fallback button identical to the primary action. Watch what happens under load.

This is what made the design problem interesting. I was designing the same feature for two completely different users — a truck owner stuck in the middle of onboarding, and an ops agent processing dozens of applications a day. The right answer for one was wrong for the other.

04

The user-facing flow — relief at the right moment

For the truck owner, the generic card has to feel like a rescue, not an option. The difference matters: an option invites comparison and deliberation. A rescue arrives when you're already stuck and just needs to get you moving again.

DECISION 01

Don't surface the fallback until the user has genuinely failed

The standard IOCL card creation flow starts with consent, moves through application details, and arrives at OTP verification. This is where 23% of users were dropping off — the OTP either didn't arrive or arrived for the wrong number.

The generic card option is not shown at the beginning of the flow, or even at the OTP screen when it first loads. It only appears after the 60-second countdown expires — as an orange banner at the bottom of the screen, below the keyboard. By that point the user has already waited, already tried, and is already frustrated. The fallback arrives as a contextually relevant solution, not a shortcut that competes with the primary flow.

The generic card banner appears only after the OTP timer expires — contextual, not competing. The user has to earn their way to it by genuinely failing first.

DECISION 02

Frame it as a feature, not a consolation prize

Once the user taps the banner, they land on a bottom sheet that leads with the IOCL logo and the headline "One step access to your new fuel card." Not "alternative card" or "limited card" — just a direct, confident description of what they're getting.

This was deliberate. The generic card works exactly like a standard IOCL card for fuel transactions. The difference — rewards accumulate under BlackBuck's CID rather than the owner's — is real, but it's a backend distinction the user won't notice in daily use. Framing the generic card as a lesser product would have been both inaccurate and damaging to conversion at the exact moment we need the user to proceed.

IOCL Generic Card bottom sheet — leads with brand authority and a benefit statement. Two options: Continue or Go Back. No hedging language.

DECISION 03

Replace what was lost — the driver number step

In the standard flow, the truck owner's mobile number becomes the OTP recipient at the petrol pump — the number the driver uses to authenticate a fuel transaction. Generic card creation skips the mobile verification entirely, which means this number never gets captured.

The solution was an "Assign Driver Number" step immediately after generic card creation. The user enters the driver's name and mobile number, verifies it with an OTP, and that number is mapped to the card as the pump authentication number. The truck owner gets a working card. The driver has a number to receive pump OTPs. Nothing breaks downstream.

Generic card created — IOCL Digital Card now shows in the truck list with a Recharge button. Assign Driver Number bottom sheet follows immediately to capture the missing mobile number.

Driver number assignment — name and mobile collected, verified with OTP. Replaces the mobile verification step that was skipped during generic card creation.

Completion — card active with Balance ₹0, OTP number and driver name visible in the card detail. Ready to recharge and use at any IOCL pump.

05

The complete flow

From the diesel homepage to a working generic card — the full sequence across both the standard path and the generic card pivot.

Entry — Diesel homepage, truck added, Add Card CTA. Bottom sheet with card options: IOCL Digital Card (tagged New), HPCL, Reliance.

Registration — IOCL consent bottom sheet, application details prefilled, two-step checklist: IOCL Registration ✓ and Truck KYC pending.

Truck KYC — Physical Card option shown, Start Truck KYC triggers phone number collection, user enters 9238657456.

06

The ops portal — same fallback, opposite design logic

The ops portal is where agents review applications and create CIDs for truck owners. 83% of cases are routine. The other 17% hit exceptions — unclear documents, minor KYC mismatches, temporary IDs — where the agent needs a way to still move the application forward.

Generic card creation exists on the portal too. But an agent is not a frustrated truck owner who's been waiting 60 seconds for an OTP. An agent is someone processing high volumes under speed pressure, with incentives tied to throughput. If the generic card path is as easy as the primary flow, it stops being a fallback and becomes the default.

DESIGN PRINCIPLE

Make exceptions possible, but not effortless

The primary CTA gets full visual weight — larger, prominent, primary colour. The generic card option sits beside it as a secondary action, smaller and visually subordinate. The hierarchy is subtle enough not to feel paternalistic, but consistent enough that agents who reach for the fallback do so with intention rather than habit.

When an agent selects the generic card route, the UI shifts into a dedicated modal. This isn't just an extra step — it's a deliberate mode switch that signals: you are now in exception handling territory. Slow down.

Inside the modal, instead of a free-text field, agents choose from a structured list of legitimate exception reasons: document unclear, temporary document, information mismatch, or other (with mandatory explanation). This accomplishes three things — it forces articulation of the actual exception, creates an audit trail, and surfaces which exception type is most common so the underlying cause can be investigated.

A final confirmation checkbox — "I confirm that I have reviewed all submitted materials" — closes the loop. It seems small. In practice it's the moment an agent shifts from passive to accountable.

MEDIUM ARTICLE The Why and How Behind Designing Exception Roads in UI Without Letting Them Become Highways ↗
07

The rewards problem — designing around a system constraint

Because generic cards sit under BlackBuck's CID, rewards earned on those transactions accumulate under BlackBuck's account in IOCL's system — not the truck owner's. IOCL increased their loyalty payout from 0.6% to 1.1% as part of this partnership, which means BlackBuck now earns up to 2.2% on generic card transactions. But the customer can't see or redeem those points in the standard rewards flow.

THE CONSTRAINT

Rewards accumulated under BlackBuck's CID cannot be made available for direct customer redemption — the system doesn't track points at card level, only at CID level. Showing unredeemable points is a trust problem.

THE SOLUTION

Block generic card rewards from the standard redemption flow. Ops team manually calculates points per card and upgrades the card limit by the equivalent amount — shown in the transaction ledger as rewards redeemed. Same visual treatment, different path to get there.

The design call here was about what not to show. Displaying a rewards balance the user can't touch would have looked like a bug — or worse, like BlackBuck withholding money. Hiding it entirely was cleaner and more honest given how the backend actually worked.

08

What this project was really about

The generic card feature isn't technically complex. The IOCL API integration is straightforward. The UI surface area is small. What made this project interesting was the design question underneath it — how do you build a responsible shortcut?

The answer was different for each user. For the truck owner, the shortcut earns its way in — it only appears after the primary path has genuinely failed. For the ops agent, the shortcut exists but costs something to use — deliberate friction in the form of structured reasons, a mode switch, and a moment of declared accountability.

Both solutions come from the same principle: the responsible action should feel natural, and the risky action should feel intentional. That balance — between usability and accountability — is what makes systems trustworthy at scale.

Not all friction is bad friction. Friction becomes harmful only when it impedes the right action. When applied to sensitive workflows, friction becomes a guide.

Dikshit Kashyap Das · 2026
